Predicting House Prices With Numpy
Replacing Python Loops with Vectorized Operations

In the previous episode, I built my first Linear Regression model using nothing but pure Python.
It worked.
The model learned from data, predicted house prices, and gave me a much deeper understanding of what happens behind model.fit().
But something bothered me.
Every calculation relied on Python loops. As the dataset grows, those loops become slower and harder to maintain.
That raised another question:
How do professional machine learning libraries train models so quickly?
The answer begins with one of the most important libraries in the Python ecosystem: NumPy.
In this episode, I’ll rebuild the same Linear Regression model using NumPy arrays and vectorized operations. The mathematics won’t change but the implementation will become cleaner, faster, and much closer to the way modern machine learning frameworks work.
The Problem
In Episode 1, we built a Linear Regression model using pure Python. Every prediction, error calculation, and weight update was performed using for loops.
While this approach helped us understand the fundamentals, it has one major limitation — it doesn’t scale well.
Imagine training a model on:

• 10 houses

• 10,000 houses

• 1 million houses
As the dataset grows, Python loops become increasingly slower, making model training less efficient.
The challenge is no longer understanding Linear Regression.
The challenge is implementing the same mathematics in a faster and more efficient way.
This is where NumPy comes in.
NumPy allows us to perform mathematical operations on entire arrays at once, eliminating the need for many explicit loops while producing the same results.
Our goal in this episode is simple:
Replace manual Python loops with NumPy’s vectorized operations without changing the underlying mathematics.
The Key Question
In the previous episode, we learned how Linear Regression works by implementing every calculation manually using Python loops.
The model worked exactly as expected.
So naturally, another question came to mind:
If the mathematics remain the same, why do professional machine learning libraries rely so heavily on NumPy instead of plain Python?
At first, I assumed NumPy was just another Python library.
It isn’t.
NumPy changes how the computations are performed, not what is being computed.
Instead of processing one value at a time, NumPy performs mathematical operations on entire arrays simultaneously. This technique, known as vectorization, significantly improves performance while producing the same predictions.
In this episode, our objective isn’t to build a better machine learning model.
Our objective is to build the same model using a more efficient engineering approach.
An Analogy
Imagine you need to calculate the total marks of 100 students.
One approach is to pick up each report card, read the marks, add them to a running total, and then move to the next student.
This is similar to how a Python for loop works, it processes one item at a time.
Now imagine giving all 100 report cards to a powerful calculator that can add every value in a single operation.
The result is exactly the same. The difference is how the work is performed. This is the idea behind NumPy vectorization.
Instead of looping through every value individually, NumPy performs mathematical operations on entire arrays at once. The mathematics remain unchanged, but the implementation becomes significantly faster, cleaner, and easier to maintain.
That is why nearly every modern machine learning library, including Scikit-Learn, TensorFlow, and PyTorch are built upon the same concept.
Theory
In Episode 1, our Linear Regression model learned three parameters:

• Area Weight

• Room Weight

• Bias
During training, the model repeatedly performed four steps:

• Make predictions

• Measure the prediction error

• Calculate the gradients

• Update the parameters
In our first implementation, every step was performed using Python for loops. We processed one training example at a time and manually accumulated the results.
NumPy allows us to represent the entire dataset as vectors and matrices instead of individual variables.
Rather than processing one house after another, we can perform calculations on all houses simultaneously.
This approach is called vectorization. The machine learning algorithm does not change. The cost function remains the same. The gradients remain the same. The learning rate remains the same.
Only the implementation changes i.e. from element-by-element calculations to matrix operations.
This is one of the biggest reasons why NumPy has become the foundation of modern scientific computing and machine learning in Python.
Mathematics
In Episode 1, we calculated the prediction for one house at a time using the Linear Regression equation:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Although the notation looks different, the mathematics is exactly the same.
The only difference is that NumPy performs the calculations for every training example simultaneously, replacing dozens of Python loop iterations with a single optimized matrix operation.
Implementation
Once the mathematics were clear, the implementation became surprisingly simple.
The biggest change from Episode 1 was not the machine learning algorithm , it was the way we represented the data.
Instead of storing each house as individual Python lists and iterating through them using for loops, we loaded the entire dataset into a NumPy array.
This allowed us to represent:

• X → Feature matrix (Area and Rooms)

• Y → Target vector (House Prices)

• W → Weight vector (Area Weight and Room Weight)
With the data stored in arrays, many operations that previously required multiple loops became a single line of code.
Step 1: Load the Dataset
We imported the CSV file and converted it directly into a NumPy array. At this point, the entire dataset became available for vectorized operations.
Step 2 : Scale the Features
Just like Episode 1, we divided the house area by 1000 before training.
Feature scaling keeps values within a similar range, allowing Gradient Descent to converge faster and preventing one feature from dominating the optimization process.
Step 3: Vectorized Predictions
Instead of predicting one house at a time, NumPy computed predictions for the entire dataset using matrix multiplication.
One operation produced predictions for every training example simultaneously.
Step 4: Vectorized Gradient Descent
Prediction errors, gradients, and parameter updates were all calculated using vector operations.
The learning algorithm remained exactly the same as Episode 1.
Only the implementation changed.
Step 5: Save the Model
After training completed, the learned parameters were stored in a JSON file containing:

• Area Weight

• Room Weight

• Bias

• Area Scale Factor
The prediction module simply loads this file and estimates the price of any new house using the trained model.
The result is cleaner code, fewer loops, and a much more efficient implementation, all without changing the underlying mathematics.
Try It Yourself
One of the best ways to understand Machine Learning is to experiment with it.
Here are a few simple changes you can make to observe how the model behaves.
Experiment 1: Increase the Learning Rate
Try increasing the learning rate from 0.001 to 0.01.
Question: Does the model converge faster, or does the training become unstable?

Experiment 2: Remove Feature Scaling
Comment out the line that scales the house area by 1000.
Question: What happens to the Area Weight and Room Weight? Can you explain why one feature begins to dominate the optimization process?

Experiment 3: Reduce the Number of Epochs
Train the model for only 100 epochs instead of 10,000.
Question: Has the model learned enough, or is it still improving?

Experiment 4: Add More Houses
Extend the dataset with a few additional houses.
Question: How do the learned weights and prediction accuracy change after retraining?

Experiment 5: Compare Version 1 and Version 2
Run the same dataset using both implementations.

• Version 1: Pure Python loops

• Version 2 : NumPy vectorization
Compare the predictions, trained weights, and execution time.
Although the results should be nearly identical, you’ll notice that the NumPy implementation is cleaner, more scalable, and significantly better suited for larger datasets.
These small experiments helped me understand that machine learning isn’t about memorizing code, it’s about observing how the model responds when we change its parameters and data.
Common Mistakes
No engineering project is complete without a few mistakes, and this one was no exception.
Here are some of the issues I encountered while building the NumPy implementation.
1. Forgetting Feature Scaling
Initially, I forgot to divide the house area by the scale factor before training.
Since the area values were much larger than the room values, the optimization process became unbalanced, causing one feature to dominate the learning process.
Lesson: Features with very different scales should usually be normalized or scaled before training.

2. Array Shape Mismatch
While converting the implementation from Python lists to NumPy arrays, I encountered shape mismatch errors during matrix multiplication.
The mathematics were correct, but the dimensions of the arrays were not.
Lesson: In NumPy, understanding the shape of your vectors and matrices is just as important as understanding the equations.

3. Treating NumPy Arrays Like Python Numbers
At one point, I attempted to use Python’s round() function directly on a NumPy array, resulting in an error.
The solution was simple, extract the scalar value before formatting the output.
Lesson: A NumPy array is an object that can contain one or many values. Even a single prediction is often returned as an array rather than a plain Python number.

Although these issues were small, solving them gave me a much deeper understanding of NumPy and how modern machine learning libraries handle data internally.
Looking back, these debugging sessions taught me more than simply writing code that worked on the first attempt.
Results
After training the model using NumPy vectorization, the learned parameters were:

• Area Weight: 155.72

• Room Weight: 21.90

• Bias: 1.58
Using these parameters, I predicted the price of a house with:

• Area: 2,200 sq ft

• Rooms: 4
The model predicted:
Predicted Price: $431.76K
The actual price in the dataset was:
Actual Price: $435.00K
The prediction differed by only $3.24K, demonstrating that the model successfully captured the relationship between the features and the target variable.
To evaluate the overall quality of the model, I also calculated the R² (Coefficient of Determination).
R² Score: 99.7%
This means the model explains approximately 99.7% of the variation in house prices using only two features:

• House Area

• Number of Rooms
While this synthetic dataset is intentionally simple, it clearly demonstrates that the NumPy implementation produces highly accurate predictions while using exactly the same machine learning algorithm as the pure Python version.
The biggest improvement in Version 2 wasn’t better mathematics, it was better engineering.
Engineering Insights
Building the same Linear Regression model twice taught me several important engineering lessons.
Mathematics and Implementation Are Different Things
The machine learning algorithm never changed, only the implementation did. Whether we use Python loops or NumPy vectorization, the mathematics behind Linear Regression remains exactly the same.

NumPy Doesn’t Make the Model Smarter
A common misconception is that using NumPy somehow improves the model. It doesn’t.
NumPy simply performs the same calculations more efficiently, allowing the code to scale to much larger datasets.

Vectorization Improves Readability
Besides being faster, vectorized code is often
